story:
Another frantic morning on Broadway in New York. Zena pushed through the crowd, repeating to herself, “I can’t be late. Not today.” Someone shouted her name. She ignored it—no time, no detours. “Zena!” Louder this time. Irritated, she turned, and a distant figure waved. As he came closer, her face shifted. That face. Could it really be him? Five years had passed since she last saw Elijah, the face that once meant everything. For three years she woke to that face in the morning light and never grew tired of it. She loved him. Fully. Surely. And yet, it ended—not from blame, not from cruelty, just life unspooling the wrong way. She remembered the day he left. She didn’t cry, didn’t beg. “This isn’t the end,” she told herself. “He’ll come back. He always does.” But she was wrong. He didn’t just leave her apartment; he left New York. He didn’t even tell her. He moved thousands of miles away.

“How could he?” she had thought while silent tears slipped down. She waited for the call that never came. She almost dialed him a hundred times—How could you do this? Did I mean anything? Was it so easy to move on?—but her pride held the line. He never reached out. She put the phone down and taught herself to stand.

Now he stood before her, smiling like nothing had happened. “Zena! It’s really you! So good to see you after so long.” She pasted on a steady voice that still trembled. “Hey, Elijah. Nice to see you too.” Five years, he said. How was she? Fine, she answered. Same old. He’d come back for a week for business, leaving tomorrow. Don’t leave. Don’t leave me again, she wanted to say. Tell him you missed him. Tell him your heart never learned the trick of forgetting. But she saw the small doll in his hand, pretty and pink. He followed her eyes. “Oh, this? For my daughter, Naomi. She won’t let me in without her dolly.” Zena smiled faintly. “She must be sweet.” Inside, she broke. She couldn’t let him see the tears gathering. “I wish I could stay, but I’m late for a meeting,” she rushed. He nodded. “Work comes first. Hope we meet again. I’d love to introduce you to my family.” I wish we never meet again, she whispered in her head. Out loud, she smiled and nodded.

She turned and walked away. Didn’t look back. Couldn’t. The pain rose with each step, heat on her cheeks in the winter cold. Five years had taught her to live and laugh without him. So why this knife of sorrow? She knew why. She had kept a small, stubborn hope alive. She dated other people, told herself the right person hadn’t come yet, but secretly she was waiting for him to return, to choose her, to find his way back. Seeing the doll, hearing “my daughter,” she felt that last fragile hope die. It hurt. It hurt terribly. But beneath the pain, something loosened. Something let go. Among the tears, a quiet relief: at last, she was free. Finally, she could move on.
